---
title: "Course Review"
subtitle: "ECON 3209 · Week 20, Lecture 3 · Kerala Agricultural University"
author: "Department of Agricultural Economics, KAU"
date: "Autumn 2026"
format:
  revealjs:
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-background-color: "#1A3300"
---

## Learning Outcomes {background-color="#1A3300"}

By the end of this lecture, you should be able to:

1. review the full 20-week structure of ECON 3209
2. summarise the most important econometric formulas and workflows from the course
3. run a compact Python mini-project that combines description, estimation, diagnostics, and forecasting
4. identify next steps for further study and research after this course
5. leave the course with a clear roadmap for independent practice

---

## Full Course Map

::: {.cols-2}
::: {}
| Week | Topic |
|---|---|
| 1 | Python Environment & Basics |
| 2 | Control Flow & Functions |
| 3 | NumPy for Economics |
| 4 | Pandas & Data Wrangling |
| 5 | Data Visualisation |
| 6 | Working with Real Data |
| 7 | Introduction to Econometrics |
| 8 | OLS: Theory & Properties |
| 9 | Inference in OLS |
| 10 | Multiple Linear Regression |
:::
::: {}
| Week | Topic |
|---|---|
| 11 | Multicollinearity |
| 12 | Heteroscedasticity |
| 13 | Dummy Variables |
| 14 | Nonlinear Regression |
| 15 | Qualitative Response Models |
| 16 | Model Specification |
| 17 | Panel Data Analysis |
| 18 | Autocorrelation |
| 19 | Time Series: Basics |
| 20 | Time Series: Forecasting |
:::
:::

---

## The Core Workflow of Applied Econometrics

1. Start with an economic question.
2. Obtain and clean data.
3. Visualise and describe the sample.
4. Choose an econometric model linked to theory.
5. Estimate, diagnose, and improve the specification.
6. Interpret results for policy, management, or research.
7. Forecast or simulate when needed.

---

## Key Formula Sheet

- OLS slope intuition: partial effect holding other regressors constant.
- Omitted variable bias:

$$\text{plim}\hat{\beta}_1 = \beta_1 + \beta_2 \frac{\text{Cov}(X_1,X_2)}{\text{Var}(X_1)}$$

- Logit probability:

$$P(Y_i=1|X_i) = \frac{e^{\beta_0+\beta_1 X_i}}{1+e^{\beta_0+\beta_1 X_i}}$$

---

## More Formulas to Remember

- Fixed effects within estimator:

$$\ddot{y}_{it} = \ddot{x}_{it}'\beta + \ddot{\varepsilon}_{it}$$

- Durbin-Watson:

$$DW = \frac{\sum_{t=2}^T(\hat{e}_t - \hat{e}_{t-1})^2}{\sum_{t=1}^T \hat{e}_t^2}$$

- ARIMA:

$$\phi(B)(1-B)^d Y_t = \theta(B)\varepsilon_t$$

---

## Mini-Project: Build a Dataset

```{pyodide-python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

rng = np.random.default_rng(2020)
n = 180
income = rng.normal(34, 7, n)
collateral = rng.binomial(1, 0.48, n)
credit_score = 60 + income + 7 * collateral + rng.normal(0, 5, n)
default = (1.4 - 0.06 * income - 0.7 * collateral - 0.02 * credit_score + rng.normal(0, 0.8, n) > 0).astype(int)
months = pd.date_range('2020-01-01', periods=n, freq='M')
npa_ratio = 12 - 0.18 * income - 1.1 * collateral - 0.05 * credit_score + 0.3 * np.arange(n) / 12 + rng.normal(0, 1.2, n)
df = pd.DataFrame({
    'month': months,
    'income': income,
    'collateral': collateral,
    'credit_score': credit_score,
    'default': default,
    'npa_ratio': npa_ratio,
})
print(df.head())
```

---

## Mini-Project: Describe the Data

```{pyodide-python}
import matplotlib; matplotlib.use('module://matplotlib_pyodide.html5_canvas_backend')
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

rng = np.random.default_rng(2020)
n = 180
income = rng.normal(34, 7, n)
collateral = rng.binomial(1, 0.48, n)
credit_score = 60 + income + 7 * collateral + rng.normal(0, 5, n)
default = (1.4 - 0.06 * income - 0.7 * collateral - 0.02 * credit_score + rng.normal(0, 0.8, n) > 0).astype(int)
months = pd.date_range('2020-01-01', periods=n, freq='M')
npa_ratio = 12 - 0.18 * income - 1.1 * collateral - 0.05 * credit_score + 0.3 * np.arange(n) / 12 + rng.normal(0, 1.2, n)
df = pd.DataFrame({'month': months, 'income': income, 'collateral': collateral, 'credit_score': credit_score, 'default': default, 'npa_ratio': npa_ratio})
print(df[['income', 'credit_score', 'npa_ratio']].describe().round(2))
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 4))
plt.hist(df['income'], bins=18, color='darkgreen', alpha=0.8)
plt.title('Income distribution in the mini-project data')
plt.show()
```

---

## Mini-Project: Estimate OLS

```{pyodide-python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

rng = np.random.default_rng(2020)
n = 180
income = rng.normal(34, 7, n)
collateral = rng.binomial(1, 0.48, n)
credit_score = 60 + income + 7 * collateral + rng.normal(0, 5, n)
default = (1.4 - 0.06 * income - 0.7 * collateral - 0.02 * credit_score + rng.normal(0, 0.8, n) > 0).astype(int)
months = pd.date_range('2020-01-01', periods=n, freq='M')
npa_ratio = 12 - 0.18 * income - 1.1 * collateral - 0.05 * credit_score + 0.3 * np.arange(n) / 12 + rng.normal(0, 1.2, n)
df = pd.DataFrame({'month': months, 'income': income, 'collateral': collateral, 'credit_score': credit_score, 'default': default, 'npa_ratio': npa_ratio})
ols = smf.ols('npa_ratio ~ income + collateral + credit_score', data=df).fit()
print(ols.summary().tables[1])
```

---

## Mini-Project: Estimate Logit

```{pyodide-python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

rng = np.random.default_rng(2020)
n = 180
income = rng.normal(34, 7, n)
collateral = rng.binomial(1, 0.48, n)
credit_score = 60 + income + 7 * collateral + rng.normal(0, 5, n)
default = (1.4 - 0.06 * income - 0.7 * collateral - 0.02 * credit_score + rng.normal(0, 0.8, n) > 0).astype(int)
df = pd.DataFrame({'income': income, 'collateral': collateral, 'credit_score': credit_score, 'default': default})
logit_model = smf.logit('default ~ income + collateral + credit_score', data=df).fit(disp=False)
print(logit_model.params.round(4))
print(logit_model.get_margeff(at='overall').summary())
```

---

## Mini-Project: Forecast a Series

```{pyodide-python}
import matplotlib; matplotlib.use('module://matplotlib_pyodide.html5_canvas_backend')
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from statsmodels.tsa.arima.model import ARIMA

rng = np.random.default_rng(2020)
n = 120
series = 100 + np.cumsum(rng.normal(0, 1.5, n))
months = pd.date_range('2015-01-01', periods=n, freq='M')
df = pd.DataFrame({'month': months, 'series': series})
model = ARIMA(df['series'], order=(1, 1, 1)).fit()
forecast = model.forecast(steps=12)
future = pd.date_range(df['month'].iloc[-1] + pd.offsets.MonthEnd(), periods=12, freq='M')
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 4))
plt.plot(df['month'], df['series'], label='Observed')
plt.plot(future, forecast, linestyle='--', marker='o', label='Forecast')
plt.legend()
plt.title('Mini-project forecasting step')
plt.show()
```

---

## What’s Next?

::: {.highlight-box}
Possible next steps after ECON 3209:
- MSc courses in econometrics, statistics, agricultural economics, or data science.
- Research projects on credit, prices, crop productivity, labour, and climate shocks.
- Internship work with banks, cooperatives, NGOs, and policy institutions.
- Independent replication of published papers using Python.
:::

---

## Research Applications

- Evaluate cooperative credit programmes.
- Model technology adoption by farmers.
- Forecast commodity prices and procurement needs.
- Measure policy effects with panel data and qualitative response models.
- Extend these tools into dissertations, term papers, and evidence-based policy work.

---

## Exercise {background-color="#1A3300"}

::: {.gold-box}
Using the mini-project starter, add one more explanatory variable or forecast comparison. Then explain which tool from the course helps you most: OLS, logit, diagnostics, panel methods, or time series.
:::

```{pyodide-python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

rng = np.random.default_rng(2021)
n = 150
income = rng.normal(35, 8, n)
collateral = rng.binomial(1, 0.5, n)
credit_score = 58 + income + 6 * collateral + rng.normal(0, 5, n)
default = (1.3 - 0.05 * income - 0.6 * collateral - 0.02 * credit_score + rng.normal(0, 0.8, n) > 0).astype(int)
df = pd.DataFrame({'income': income, 'collateral': collateral, 'credit_score': credit_score, 'default': default})
print(smf.logit('default ~ income + collateral + credit_score', data=df).fit(disp=False).params)
```

---

## Summary {background-color="#1A3300"}

- ✅ The course moved from Python foundations to core econometric tools and finally forecasting methods.
- ✅ You now have a workflow for data cleaning, visualisation, estimation, diagnosis, and interpretation.
- ✅ The most important habit is to connect methods to economic questions and data structure.
- ✅ With continued practice, these tools can support exams, projects, research, and professional work.

---

## Thank You {background-color="#1A3300"}

::: {.highlight-box}
**ECON 3209 · Econometrics with Python**  
Department of Agricultural Economics  
Kerala Agricultural University

- Thank you for your energy, questions, and coding effort across 20 weeks.
- Keep practising with real Kerala data: crop yields, prices, credit, and rural livelihoods.
- Best wishes for your exams, projects, and future research.
:::
